content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 23, 1987

William D. Esselstein
Robertson, Alexander, Luther,
Esselstein, Shiells & Wright
750 Menlo Avenue, Suite 250
Menlo Park, CA 94025

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our Advice File No. I-87-180

Dear Mr. Esselstein:

You have contacted us regarding the California Sanitation
Risk Management Association's need to develop a conflict of
interest code under the Political Reform Act.1/ You are the
legal counsel to the California Sanitation Risk Management
Association (CSRMA), a recently formed joint powers agency.
The agency is comprised of approximately 40 public agencies and
intends to pool its funds for the purpose of providing a
self-insurance program.

You have asked for assistance in developing a conflict of
interest code for the agency and, more particularly, help in
designing appropriate disclosure categories.

As you are aware, the Fair Political Practices Commission
is the code-reviewing body for local governmental agencies with
jurisdiction in more than one county. (Section 82011(a).) As
such, we have developed a regulation which describes the
procedures that agencies are to follow in promulgating their
conflict of interest codes. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18750.1,
copy enclosed.)

The basic process for adopting a code is to:

1. Prepare the proposed code and a written notice
describing the code;

2. Provide notice to employees for comment on the code;

_____________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

William D. Esselstein
July 17, 1987
Page 2


     3.    Forty-five days after notice, conduct a public hearing
           on the code;

     4.    After formal adoption, submit the code to the Fair
           Political Practices Commission for approval.

     The specific instructions are contained in the enclosed
regulation.

     I have included samples of conflict of interest codes for
joint powers agencies with structures similar to yours.
Perhaps the language developed for these agencies could be
modified or altered for your code.

     After you have reviewed the attached, please feel free to
call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  Jeanette Turvill
                                             Legal Assistant

DMG:JET:jaj
Enclosures

MYRON G ALEXANDER
JACK ROBERTSON
JAMES LUTHER
WILLIAM D ESSELSTEIN
DAVID C SHIELS
TIMOTHY C WRIGHT
KINGSFORD F JONES
MARSDEN S BLOIS
ELIZABETH JACOBS BOYLE
DIANE S GREENBERG
RUSSELL L BOHNE
NANCY F TEMPLETON

ROBERTSON, ALEXANDER, LUTHER, ESSELSTEIN, SHIELS & WRIGHT
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
750 MENLO AVENUE, SUITE 250
MENLO PARK, CALIFORNIA 94025
(415) 324-0622

SAN JOSE OFFICE
SUITE 540
CROCKER BANK BUILDING
64 WEST SANTA CLARA STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
(408) 286-9700

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL #
(415) 324-

June 16, 1987

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: California Sanitation Risk Management Association

Ladies/Gentlemen:

I am general legal counsel to California Sanitation Risk Management
Association ("CSRMA"), a recently formed joint exercise of powers
authority. CSRMA was formed by approximately 40 California public
agencies to develop jointly operated and administered insurance and
self-insurance programs. Membership is restricted to public agencies
engaged in sewerage collection and treatment activities. At present,
CSRMA is operating a general liability insurance program in which
about 21 agencies are participants.

It was recently recognized that it would be necessary to develop a
Conflict of Interest Code meeting the requirements of the Political
Reform Act. Under the Act, because CSRMA is a statewide agency, the
Code must be approved by the Fair Political Practices Commission.
However, the Board of Directors of CSRMA is unclear as to the form
the Code should take in order to satisfy the FPPC. Of particular
concern is the definition of appropriate disclosure categories.
Although all Board Members are familiar with the disclosure categories
applicable to the agencies they represent, these do not seem suited
to the types of activities in which CSRMA engages.

The purpose of this letter is to ask the assistance of the FPPC staff
in attempting to formulate an acceptable code. Because I will be
absent on an extended vacation through July 20th, please contact my
partner, Diane Greenberg.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM D. ESSELSTEIN

WDE:bls:417

cc: CSRMA Executive Committee

sections.question_synthetic: What procedures should the California Sanitation Risk Management Association follow to develop a conflict of interest code, and what disclosure categories are appropriate for a joint powers agency engaged in self-insurance pooling?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides guidance on the four-step process for adopting a conflict of interest code (prepare code, provide employee notice, conduct public hearing, submit to FPPC for approval) and offers sample codes from similar joint powers agencies that can be modified for CSRMA's specific structure and activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated June 16, 1987) and the FPPC response letter (dated July 23, 1987). The response provides procedural guidance and references to regulations (2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18750.1) rather than answering a specific legal question. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present; synthesized summaries derived from letter content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What procedures should the California Sanitation Risk Management Association follow to develop a conflict of interest code, and what disclosure categories are appropriate for a joint powers agency engaged in self-insurance pooling?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provides guidance on the four-step process for adopting a conflict of interest code (prepare code, provide employee notice, conduct public hearing, submit to FPPC for approval) and offers sample codes from similar joint powers agencies that can be modified for CSRMA's specific structure and activities.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a request for assistance from legal counsel for the California Sanitation Risk Management Association regarding development of a conflict of interest code, providing procedural guidelines and sample codes from similar joint powers agencies.
